---
title: "Evaluation Guide"
---

## Evaluate against fitness for purpose

Address these prompts:

- Who is the intended audience?
- How does the website meet their needs?
- Which design choices were most effective?
- Which features did not work as well as expected?

## Evaluate your development process

- What testing methods did you use? (self-test, peer test, checklist)
- What specific feedback changed your outcome?
- What evidence shows iteration over time?

## Example reflective paragraph starter

> My website was designed for Year 11 students learning about environmental trends. I used a simple navigation layout and short sections so users could quickly compare rainfall, PM2.5, and river nitrate results. Peer testing showed my first graph labels were unclear, so I updated axis titles, improved contrast, and added short interpretations under each graph. This improved usability because users could understand the data patterns without teacher help.

## A/M/E evidence reminder

Use the **A/M/E Evidence** page to model how you write your final evidence statements.
